item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait. So there are two Gaia’s? I’m referring to SCP-4043.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could stand to be updated given everything else that has happened since.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is to be assumed as non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great concept and even better execution. I love the ambiguity and the development of the SCP. My only gripe is that I don't like the way that the revisions work. I feel like strikethroughs and color-coded text could portray two series of revisions far better than what amounts to two entire repeats of the containment procedures and description with a few sentences of additions tops. Still a +1, but I do think the revision history could be done better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm with Travalgar on this one. It eludes me how it got this upvoted.

The main idea isn't bad, mind you, but there are some problems that couldn't bring me to not downvote.

Mostly, the characters overreact here. It is a liquid that turns plants and animals into human predators. So what? The article spends too much words saying it is uncontrollably dangerous, but simply don't show it. Lock it up in a freezer and the thing can be pretty much forgotten for all that I saw. Test with some D-Classes in one of the several air-tight isolated chambers of the Foundation.

And the Gaia Hypothesis is pretty much hammered. The article shows no reason to reach such conclusion. Not finding who did it doesn't mean no one did it, there should be some evidence of this thing developing spontaneously.

Seriously, I understand that the format of the article is good, but at its current state, I really prefer it to be either rewritten or deleted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For your information, I do read the whole article of every skips I read, and sadly I fail to come to the same conclusion. To me, it could have been just two radical environmentalists giving us the wrong ideas. The execution is not smooth enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great, just great. Jumanji death plants now exist. Well, my day is effectively ruined!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think you read the whole thing, the plot twist is that it isn't created by some group, it's created by EARTH ITSELF, which means the earth wants us dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I, for one, cannot understand why this SCP is particularly well upvoted. So it's something that makes plants and animals rebel against human. So, what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This being one of the first SCPs I read way back in 2011 is what convinced me to keep reading. The ambiguity of the situation and what's going on, as well as a natural evolution of an article over time, leads to a read that grows more and more interesting over time. The note at the end is probably a bit hammy; however, given the strengths of everything else, it seems charming in its own way. Easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't mind the fact that this is the serum of nature weaponization, but I feel that the iterations weren't as good as they could have been, given how clearly iterative they were.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It could have been some GOI influencing a researcher and telling him that it's the "Earth's immune system" when it isn't, to get him to let the SCP go free

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great article! I do like the bit at the end, but I agree with XenocideSoldier on the fact it was a bit spelled out at the end. You should have left it to the reader's knowledge to whom Gaia was. Other then that, it was a great article, and I liked the way you included a back story of it's time in containment.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm…. I think it was spelled out a bit too clearly in the end bit, I had already got a little sense of what you were going for just from the article, but that file at the end just spoiled it for me.
Some kind of exploration log from when/where they first found it seems like it would be a bit more fitting, and with the potential to make it a lot meatier.
No vote from me for now.

item.title: Interesting
item.content:encoded: So it's a chamber that makes plants wacked up +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great idea, excellent execution. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goddamnit I was reading this on my bed next to my cat and then it said the bit about what happens to pets and I look over at her and she just stares back and I swear to god she SMILED A CAT CAN'T EVEN FUCKING SMILE WHAT THE BLOODY FUCK

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … In case that's not sarcasm/bad joke, it was Aelanna.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't figure out who wrote this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the idea is to make it easier to spot the adaptations of the SCP quickly and to make it clear how it has progressed from Safe to Euclid to Keter.

That said, collapsibles break the flow of a document and make it hard to turn in to a pdf (or so I've been told).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like how the "Old" documents are almost entirely the same thing, with about two lines of changed text. The format could use some work, but I like the idea. If the format becomes less redundant, Ill upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel absolutely awful for saying this, because a) this is really well-written and b) I never even watched the show that I'm gonna say this reminded me of, but… uh… this was dangerously close to Captain Planet territory for me. I'M SORRY I'M SORRY *leaves without voting*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although the execution is solid, this one reminds me too much of SCP-1000. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't help but feel that this doesn't feel original. I know it varies somewhat from other plant and wildlife SCPs to the point that they are different, but it just feels like the effects are a combination of a few SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my favorite SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A solid idea, with a few extra bits (like descriptions of what happened at BCS-26) would make it even better. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should read "unsuitable for human consumption" rather than "dangerous for human consumption". Other than that… It's kinda meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the format. Very unique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Worth an up vote to me. Only thing that got me was the structuring of the document. Felt a little weird, having multiple hidden sections describing amendments to the containment procedures and such. Granted, this is because I'm used to the alternative approach, which is to strike out the previous procedures, and follow with the amendments.

Otherwise, I like the concept. I liked the ambiguity as to who or what was responsible for creating the substance. Adds a bit of a creep factor to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The funny thing is, the logs neither confirm nor deny this, in the minds of some. Some crazy environmentalist researchers simply chose to believe the latter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, I have upvoted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
evacuated, reinforced containment chamber at Site █.

Do you mean vacuum evacuated, or some other sort of evacuated?

composed primarily of heavy proteins

I believe that should be "large proteins", not "heavy proteins".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually like the idea that this was engineered by crazy environmentalists better than the Gaia explanation, but that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, this is bloody awesome.The SCP itself is just about allright, but the containment/description revisions, and the incident log are bloody well written. If I could upvote it twice, I would.

In other news, foundation personnel undergoing mental breakdown, hmm. For some reason, it makes me wonder whether hostility to other people of that sort wouldn't be unexplored effect of human absorbtion of the substance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: KUDZZZUUUUUUU!!!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get that sometimes, but the Gaia Hypothesis was around long before that god-awful movie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I may be biassed, but this seems pretty similar to SCP-299. Mind you, the execution on this one is a lot better. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get that this probably has nothing to do with The Happening, but it's too reminiscent for me, sorry.
